claim: Mainstream financial-research firms are publishing AI infrastructure-spending forecasts at scale unprecedented in U.S. economic history. Goldman Sachs' baseline model estimates $765 billion in annual U.S. AI capital expenditure in 2026, rising to $1.6 trillion annually by 2031, with AI-related capex already representing approximately 5 percent of U.S. GDP. Dell'Oro Group projects global data-center capex to grow at a 21 percent CAGR, reaching $1.2 trillion worldwide by 2029. Citi forecasts that Amazon, Apple, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Oracle collectively will spend $382 billion in 2026, $432 billion in 2027, and $539 billion in 2029 on capex. The Bank for International Settlements has documented that lending by private credit funds to AI-related sectors has grown from near zero to over $200 billion outstanding, with projections of $300–600 billion by 2030.

sources.label: Goldman Sachs — “Tracking Trillions: The Assumptions Shaping the Scale of the AI Build-Out”
Bank for International Settlements — “Financing the AI boom: from cash flows to debt” (BIS Bulletin 120)
Data Center Dynamics — “Global data center capex projected to grow at 21 percent CAGR to $1.2tn by 2029”
sources.quote: global data center capex projected to grow at 21 percent CAGR to $1.2tn by 2029

sources.label: S&P Global Market Intelligence — “Private equity investment surge sends US data center deals to 5-year high”
Future AI / @future.is.ai, Instagram reel DWxV_gPD_DH (Apr 5, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: These figures come from named institutional research, not from advocacy sources. They establish the numerical scale that the Priestley 2029 prediction (2026-priestley-2029-prediction-001) is reasoning about. The relevant editorial question is whether the hardware-obsolescence cycle, the user-monetization gap, and the private-credit exposure together amount to a structural mismatch.
